In the Name of God, the Most Compassionate, the Most Merciful A letter addressed to all Iranians, inside and outside the country, especially to politi
Dear Iranians, As an Iranian, despite all the hardships I have faced on this path—from the unavoidable leap I had to take from a window on September 2
This book is a covenant that unveils the historical secrets of religion and Iran, and brings all wars and disputes to peace—and I have no doubt about
To prove the sincerity of my conduct, I remained in Iran under the harshest conditions of an uncertain life and during the most difficult days of my c
For every word of this book and this path, I did not use a single rial—or even a single kilobyte—that was wrongful or forbidden.
This book belongs to the civilization of Iran, and it had to be written within the civilization of Iran and born on Iranian soil.
I have written a theory and proposed a plan in which every Iranian, with any religion or lack of religion, any language, ethnicity, political inclinat
This theory begins from a single principle: the acceptance of human free will; a principle that everyone, even when disagreeing with one another, is i
From this point, step by step, I have arrived at Freedom, individual property rights, religion, Iran, law, economics, governance, and practical soluti
Iran holds within itself a secret as vast as history.
This historical battle began in Iran, and it will also end in Iran—not merely through war and politics, but through the truth of Freedom, religion,
This book is an effort for Iran to become Iran once again; for religion to become religion once again; for Freedom to leave the realm of slogans and b
For the rebirth of the civilization of Iran, its theory has been born.
If there is any truth in it, it belongs to Iran; it belongs to all Iranians who do not want the future of this land to be buried in hatred, bloodshed,
This book is the new world order founded upon the civilization of Iran, and by the end you will understand what indescribable power the civilization o
This book exposes the hidden secrets of Iran.
It is time for Iranians to turn this theory from text into action, from thought into institution, and from book into civilization.
It is time for Iranians, through this very book, to shape the future of Iran by the hands of Iranians.
Iran has room—in its land, culture, thought, and tolerance—for all Iranians, inside and outside the country.
This is the beginning of Iran’s new history.

Liberty is not hierarchical and pyramidal; rather, it is networked and full of texture.

institution such as the central bank, and liberty becomes very clear.

A Journey into the Depths of the Meaning of Life Introductory Chapter: A Journey into the Depths of Liberty, Religion, Iran, and the Future of Humanit
A wound that has been named, at different times, despotism, backwardness, poverty, the distortion of religion, Westoxication, Eastoxication, the crisi
Yet in this book I seek to show that the root of many of these scattered names lies in a deeper problem: the loss of the meaning of liberty.
Throughout history, liberty has been one of the most frequently used and, at the same time, most ambiguous words in human discourse.
Everyone has spoken of liberty—states, revolutionaries, philosophers, mystics, intellectuals, kings, parties, schools of thought, empires, and even op
But whenever liberty lacks an objective and judgeable definition, the very word that is meant to save humanity becomes an instrument of enslavement.
Power first defines liberty, then imposes that definition upon human beings, and finally calls enslavement “liberty.” This book begins precisely at th
Liberty must be definable, measurable, agreeable, formally expressible, and defensible.
Liberty must not remain a poetic, emotional, mystical, political, or propagandistic state.
Liberty must become something such that, if it is violated, the violation can be demonstrated; if it is preserved, it can be explained; and if someone
My answer in this book is clear: liberty, in its objective form, is nothing other than individual property rights.
By property, however, I do not mean merely ownership of a house, land, money, or goods.
Property begins with the human body—with life, will, time, labor, thought, reputation, family, contracts, achievements, land, livelihood, choice, and
A human being who has no right to defend his property, family, speech, belief, contracts, and will is not free.
A human being who must receive his livelihood, security, meaning, and law from the state is on the path to enslavement—even if that enslavement is cal
It is here that the question of religion reopens.
In this book, I do not seek to separate religion from liberty; on the contrary, I seek to show that true religion—if purified of mysticism, state-wors
Religion, in its essence, did not come to enslave human beings to the state; it did not come to demean human property; it did not come to devalue the
Religion came to liberate the human being from servitude to anything other than God.
Islam, in this reading, is not merely an identity label or a collection of rituals.
Islam means submission to God; and submission to God, at the social level, means submission to the rights that God has established for His servants.
Even salaam is not merely a verbal greeting; it is a declaration of proprietary peace: I will not violate your life, property, body, will, reputation,
If this is so, religion is not the enemy of liberty, but the covenant of liberty.
But if religion is such, why is our history filled with religious states, religious repression, religious distortions, sanctified poverty, obedience t
The answer of this book has one key word: mysticism.
Mysticism here does not mean merely chanting, Sufi lodges, poetry, emotional fervor, or personal experience.
Mysticism is the name for any intellectual or political system that separates the human being from property, body, land, reason, revelation, the marke
Sometimes mysticism appears in religious garb, sometimes in philosophical garb, sometimes as intellectualism, sometimes as socialism, sometimes as nat
The criterion for recognizing it is simple: wherever human property, human liberty, free will, and the human right over body, wealth, reputation, and
Wherever the material world is declared worthless, land is considered base, the body is humiliated, welfare, the market, and productive activity are p
In this book, I seek to show that the fundamental human conflict is not between religion and irreligion; it is between religion and mysticism.
It is the conflict between revelation and fabricated revelation; between property and appropriation; between liberty and the state; between the human
From here, Iran enters the scene.
Iran in this book is not merely a geography, a state, a political border, or a historical memory.
Iran is a civilizational continuum—a land that, if it returns to its truth, can once again reconstruct the relationship among liberty, faith, property
Iran, in this reading, is the land of proprietors; and because it is the land of proprietors, it is the land of free people.
Property is a divine right of the human being; therefore, the foundation of liberty rests upon faith.
But Iran has been wounded from two sides: on one side, religion has been separated from liberty and turned into an instrument of the state, power, obe
The result of this separation has been a false war between being Iranian and being religious—as if Iran and religion were enemies, when in their true
A war that is not merely political; it is epistemological, methodological, historical, theological, economic, and now, with the emergence of artificia
In the past, one could claim religion, ethics, liberty, or justice through rhetoric, attire, titles, power, fame, tradition, or arbitrary interpretati
But the age of artificial intelligence is the age of testing consistency.
If humanity cannot write a finite, clear, axiomatic, Consistent, and executable ethical system for machines, machines will either serve totalitarian s
Here the theory of liberty presents its challenge: if true religion is a consistent formal system for defending liberty and individual property rights
If one interpretation of religion can operate on a machine in a Consistent manner, while another cannot even produce a few non-contradictory propositi
This question is not directed only at a government, a seminary, or a sect; it is directed at all claimants of religion, philosophy, ethics, politics,
If you claim a connection to the Hidden Imam (may God hasten his reappearance), then obtain from him and present a consistent, axiomatic, Consistent f
If you claim that only you understand religion, solve this problem on the machine.
If you cannot present a Consistent formal interpretation or system derived from religion to solve the ethical and legal problems of artificial intelli
Consequently, until then, you cannot raise a structural, methodological, or epistemic objection to the theory of Liberty–Religion– Iran.
If you claim a connection to God, present a consistent formal ethical system for artificial intelligence like the one extracted from religion in this
In any case, God’s miracle has been of the nature of a book—precisely for such a day, when truth may be distinguished from falsehood—and this book, on
For now, however, read the book to the end, for the good of Iran and religion requires that it be read at least once.
For the entire image of religion and Iran has been inverted; first the correct image must be seen as a whole, and only then should one attend to the d
From here, the book enters its main arena: returning religion to the form of a consistent formal system.
Not a religion to frighten people; not a religion for the state; not a religion for a sect; not a religion to sanctify poverty; not a religion to deny
It is the gradual discovery of a hidden relationship: the relationship among liberty, religion, Iran, property, the state, mysticism, reason, revelati
Along this path, the reader may initially encounter topics that seem unrelated: the family, the central bank, mysticism, Hegel, Gödel, artificial inte
The family suddenly connects to the central bank; property to revelation; religion to the market; Iran to liberty; artificial intelligence to prophecy
My covenant with the reader is simple: wherever you see the theory of liberty, individual property rights, or free will violated, stop there.
Wherever you see betrayal of Iran, stop.
Wherever you see distortion of religion, stop.
But if you see that each step connects liberty to property, property to
rights, rights to revelation, revelation to religion, religion to Iran, and Iran to the future of humanity, then remain with me to the end.
For this book seeks to show that the issue is not merely the salvation of Iran; it is the salvation of the meaning of liberty.
The issue is not merely criticism of the state; it is the return of law to the limits of rights.
The issue is not merely defending religion; it is separating religion from mysticism and state-worship.
A religion without a non-contradictory formal structure becomes an instrument of power.
A liberty without an objective definition becomes a pretext for enslavement.
And a human being who does not recognize his own property will, sooner or later, become the property of the state.
This book is an invitation to return: a return to religion—but not state religion; a return to Iran— but not a confiscated Iran; a return to liberty—b
This book begins with one claim and seeks, throughout its course, to clarify it step by step: True religion is the covenant of liberty; liberty is the
If you feed all parts of this book to artificial intelligence, it will produce axiomatic tagging and structuring—similar to what has been done for som
I did not have the opportunity to complete this fully, but these tags and argumentative paths are what map the entire book as a large graph of axiomat
Covenant with the Reader before Beginning the Journey From the reader of this book I ask for neither blind faith, nor immediate acceptance, nor emotio
This work rests on a simple yet weighty claim: If Liberty is understood objectively, it leads us to individual property rights; if individual property
I do not ask the reader to accept anything in the name of religion, Iran, Liberty, Shi‘ism, reason, logic, or artificial intelligence.
Wherever you see a violation of Liberty, stand still.
Wherever you see a violation of individual property rights, stand still.
Wherever you find that I have enslaved the human being to power under the name of religion, stop me there.
Wherever you find that I have turned Iran into a pretext for domination, you must stop me there.
The theory of Liberty is not a single proposition; it is a network.
Each concept here clarifies another: Liberty by property, property by right, right by revelation, revelation by religion, religion by the rejection of
The reader may initially ask why a book on Liberty speaks of religion; later, why a book on religion speaks of property; and then see how the discussi
At first glance this breadth may look like dispersion; in reality, Liberty itself is like that.
Liberty is not a linear, one-dimensional topic—it is a web that connects to the body, family, market, State, religion, language, history, land, money,
It is a journey—from ambiguity to objectivity, from slogan to formal system, from vague Liberty to individual property rights, from property to the di
If the reader stops at the first encounter with an odd statement and forms a final judgment before seeing the whole structure, he risks repeating the
When it speaks of Liberty, it does not mean the feeling of release—it means individual property rights.
When it speaks of religion, it does not mean state religion, mystical religion, sectarian religion, or power-oriented religion—it means the formal sys
When it speaks of Iran, it does not mean an established government, a particular ruling class, ethnic group, language, or ideology—it means the civili
And when it speaks of mysticism, it does not merely mean a spiritual vocabulary or historical figures—it means any system that alienates man from prop
Fairness If the reader opposes religion, I ask him not to judge until I have shown that the religion under discussion is not the statist, anti-Liberty
If he opposes Liberty, I ask whether Liberty without property has any objective meaning at all.
If he distrusts private property, I ask him to see whether responsibility, civilization, family, market, and Liberty can endure without it.
If he distrusts Iranian identity, I ask him to see whether Iran here is a pretext for state worship or a bastion of Liberty.
If he is religious, I ask him to see whether a religion that defends Liberty, human rights, property, fidelity, and rejection of servitude to anything
This book asks the reader to distinguish between: religion and what has been imposed upon him in its name; Iran and what has been appropriated in its
I will show that: Liberty without property turns into a slogan.
Property without divine origin cannot withstand the State, the majority, or power.
Religion without Liberty becomes corrupted.
Iran without a liberating religion is left without a shield.
Reason without revelation slips into subjectivism, dialectic, and mysticism.
Revelation reduced to inner experience or personal inspiration becomes mysticism again.
And any civilization lacking finite, non-contradictory axioms collapses under its own contradictions.
Thus, this book is not written only for believers; it is also for lovers of Liberty.
Not only for those attached to Iran; but also for those who wonder why Iran remains a global question.
Not only for critics of the State; but also for those who seek to know why the State, once it crosses the bounds of property, becomes the political em
Not only for philosophers; but for those who wish to move from philosophy to formal system.
Not only for the devout; but for those who wish to understand why a correctly understood religion is the most precise shield of man against servitude.
In this path, liberalism and socialism are not the end of debate—they are only its first layers.
Theism and atheism are not the end either, for a god produced by mysticism, dialectic, or the human mind may still violate Liberty.
The ultimate opposition is revelation vs. mysticism.
The real conflict is not between religion and irreligion—it is between religion and the falsification of religion.
The final conflict is Liberty vs. the State, property vs. slavery, prophetic revelation vs. unbounded inner vision, man as God’s vicegerent vs. man as
Hence the reader will encounter many critiques: of socialism, modern liberalism, the welfare state, mysticism, dialectical philosophies, Hegel, state
On the constructive side, it aims to show: how Liberty can be founded upon individual property rights; how property rights can be traced to a divine s
Throughout, I ask the reader to keep only one principle in mind: wherever individual property rights are violated, Liberty is violated; wherever Liber
Holding this criterion to the end, you will see that many things once thought separate suddenly connect: why money leads to religion; why family leads
This book makes a covenant with its reader: I will strive never to introduce an axiom without reason, never to leave a concept undefined, never to det
In return, I ask the reader not to rush, not to fear familiar words, not to prejudge upon hearing “religion,” “Iran,” “property,” “Shi‘ism,” or “artif
from dispersion to consistency, from distortion to decipherment, from state-worship to Liberty, from mysticism to revelation, from ambiguity to formal
It is a path—a path that begins with a simple question and leads to a reconstruction of the relationship among liberty, religion, Iran, property, the
If at the beginning some chapters appear scattered, the reason is not a lack of consistency in the theory; the reason is the networked nature of liber
Liberty is not merely a political issue.
It is connected to the body, family, property, money, the market, religion, the state, history, language, Iran, revelation, mysticism, artificial inte
At first, I thought the main conflict was the dispute between liberalism and socialism.
Later I realized the issue ran deeper and concerned the relationship between religion and liberty.
Then it became clear that even belief in God alone was not sufficient, because theism—if mixed with mysticism, dialectics, pantheism, statism, or mind
Eventually, the problem reached its clearest dual form: revelation or mysticism; religion or the fabrication of religion; liberty or the state; proper
After this gradual introduction, the book turns to the definition of liberty.
In this section, I show that if liberty is not made objective, it becomes a dangerous word.
Anyone can define liberty however they wish and impose that definition on others.
To escape this danger, liberty must become something that can be agreed upon, measured, and formally articulated.
At this point, liberty is grounded in individual property rights.
In this book, property does not refer only to material possessions; it includes ownership of one’s body, life, will, time, labor, reputation, family,
After defining liberty, the book turns to religion.
Here I argue that true religion is not the enemy of liberty but the most precise system for defending it.

The Three Core Functions Suffolk Performed •
Private Clearinghouse: settling mutual claims among banks.
Monetary Supervisor: enforcing discipline so no bank could inflate recklessly.
Market-wide Credibility Enhancer: ensuring that a member bank’s notes were accepted at full value across New England.
Why Printing Money Was Not “Free” It might seem that, since any bank could print its own notes, reckless expansion would be inevitable.
But printing money in this system was costly.
Notes came back quickly for redemption, and the issuing bank had to present real reserves on demand.
In plain terms: printing banknotes was easy; enduring their redemption was hard—and that difficulty imposed restraint.

A reply to the mystical critique that asks: Why should we love a God who threatens?
Within the mystical traditions of Monadology, wahdat al-wujūd (unity of existence), and Gnosticism, it is posited that the true God requires no fear.
Consequently, Allah—God forbid—is characterized as a neurotic, violent entity, ultimately identified with the Demiurge.
However, this assertion represents precisely one of the Devil’s stratagems for the annihilation of Liberty.
We shall address this with the same axiomatic reasoning employed in the discourse on the abolition of chattel slavery.
The crux of the matter is not whether God necessitates our fear.
Rather, it is that we require the singular object of our fear to be God alone, to the exclusion of all else.
Should human fear not be directed toward the True God, it will not dissipate; instead, it will merely disseminate throughout the social fabric.
In such a scenario, individuals, rather than fearing the Self-Sufficient God, will succumb to fearing the state, the spiritual guide, the collective,
A society permeated by diffused fear ceases to be a society of Liberty.
Such a society, forsaking the defense of Freedom, will pursue merely the most minimal form of survival.
Therefore, within the dialectic between religion and mysticism, this fear must be purposefully channeled to become an advocate for Liberty, the rights
If we do not fear the self-sufficient God alone, our fears do not disappear; they are merely transferred to other beings and other human beings.
Fear is part of the human being’s rational and biological mechanism.
If there is no fear of God, fear is not removed; it is dispersed.
States seek to de-religionize society so that people will not fear God, but instead will have their fear concentrated on the state itself — the earthl
This is where God-fearingness, and fearing only God, becomes the guarantee of liberty and happiness.
For the human being who fears only God no longer bows before any other earthly god.
Not before the state, not before the guide, not before the sect, not before the majority, and not before any power that seeks to take God’s place.

So fear of God is not anti-liberty; it is the foundation of liberty.
It is the only way to gather fear away from the earthly gods and concentrate it upon a transcendent, self-sufficient point.
For God does not need us to fear Him; rather, we need to fear only Him.

Whereas the correct answer is this: God has no need to frighten anyone; it is the human being who, in order to remain free, must fear only God.
And that is the root of slavery.

Loss, Bankruptcy, and the Right to Exit An enterprise that remains unprofitable after the transfer of ownership should not be bailed out with budgets,
Bankruptcy is part of the market order because it shifts resources from bad uses to better ones.

Executive Sequence for the Specification of Enterprise Ownership The proposed sequence for enterprises is as follows: 1.
Publish a complete list of state, public, quasi-state, and semi-state enterprises. 2.
Clarify the ownership of land, debt, assets, contracts, human resources, and obligations for each unit. 3.
Categorize the enterprises into industrial, educational, medical, financial, mining, energy, service, and infrastructure sectors. 4.
Define the baseline ownership of each unit for the actual employees of that unit. 5.
Prohibit sales to state, semi-state, quasi-state, banking, and institutional legal entities. 7.
Create a limited internal market for the liquidity of employee shares. 9.
Remove enterprises from pricing mandates, redundant licenses, monopolies, and ministerial interference. 12.
Enable legal bankruptcy and voluntary restructuring. 14.
Allow enterprises to operate with free money, multi-currency contracts, proprietary energy, and a free market. 15.

Condensed Final Order of the Rescue Package for Iran The final order is as follows: 1.
Declaration of the fundamental principle of property rights and prohibition of administrative shock. 2.
Stabilization of non-disputed ownership and referral of disputes to competent courts and arbitration. 3.
Transparency of the state’s assets, debts, obligations, and accumulated losses. 4.
Creation of a legal infrastructure for secure transfer of ownership. 5.
Preliminary liberalization of markets, contracts, entry and exit, prices, and trade. 6.
Freedom of money, currencies, multi-currency contracts, and the beginning of free banking. 7.
Clarification of energy ownership, creation of an energy-share market, and non-administrative discovery of energy prices. 8.
Clarification of land ownership and spatial commons before transferring enterprises. 9.
Assignment of ownership of state and public enterprises to the employees and managers of those same units under specified conditions. 10.
Resolution of unhealthy banks, pension funds, and accumulated losses without imposing shock on the people. 11.
Elimination of anti-production taxes and replacement with limited common costs. 12.
Immediate removal of tariffs or reduction to a single very low fixed rate and full freedom of foreign trade. 13.
Removal of monopolies over sanction-circumvention under free trade. 14.
Liberation of education, health, science, and religion from the state.
Two-stage downsizing of the state and limiting it to defense, adjudication, property registration, contract enforcement, and security. 16.

If prices are liberalized before ownership is clarified, the result is shock.
If enterprises are transferred before money, land, and debts are clarified, ownership remains incomplete.
If energy prices rise before ownership shares are defined, the result is unrest and distrust.
If banks are rescued before money is freed, inflation returns.
If the state shrinks but the Fundamental Law does not prevent its return, it grows again.

The Iran Rescue Package is nothing other than this: pushing the state back from its godlike position and returning human beings to their own ownership
